utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: hi , john , how are you doing ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: hi , good to see you .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: good to see you too . so i know the nurse told you about dax , i'd like to tell dax a little about you .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: sure .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: so john is a 55-year-old male with a past medical history significant for anxiety and epilepsy who presents with an abnormal lab finding . so , john , um , i , uh , was notified by the emergency room that you , um , had a really high blood sugar and you were in there with , uh ... they had to treat you for that , what was going on ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yeah , we've been going from place to place for different events and we've had a lot of visitors over the last couple of weeks and i just was n't monitoring my sugar intake and , uh , a little too much stress and strain i think over the last couple of weeks .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay , yeah , i had gone through your hemoglobin a1c's and you know , they were borderline in the past but-

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: mm-hmm

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: -i guess , you know , i guess they're high now so how are you feeling since then ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: so far so good .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay , did they put you on medication ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: uh , they actually did .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay , all right . i think they have here metformin ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yeah , that's- that sounds right .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: all right , um , and , um , in terms of your anxiety , i'm sure that this did n't help much-

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: did n't help , no , not at all .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: how are you doing with that ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: um , i had my moments but , um , it ... now that it's almost the weekend , it's- it's been a little bit better . i think things are under control by now .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: okay .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay ? um , how about your epilepsy , any seizures recently ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: not in a while , it's been actually quite a few months and it was something minor but noth- nothing major ever since .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay . all right , well you know i wanted to just go ahead and do , um , a quick review of the systems , i know you did a cheat with the nurse-

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: mm-hmm .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: any chest pain , shortness of breath , nausea , vomiting , dizzy- dizziness ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: no , no .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay , any recent fever , chills ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: no .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay . and all right , let's go ahead do a quick physical exam . hey , dragon , show me the vitals . so looking here at your vital signs today , um , they look really good . so i'm just gon na go ahead and take a listen to your heart and lungs .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: mm-hmm .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay , so on physical examination , you know , everything seems to look really good , um lungs are nice and clear , your heart's at a regular rate and rhythm . you do have some trace pitting edema to your lower extremities so what that means is that it looks like you might be retaining a little bit of fluid-

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: mm-hmm .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: um , did they give you a lot of fluid in the emergency room ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: they actually did .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay , all right , so it might just be from that . okay , well let's look at some of your results . hey , dragon , show me the glucose . okay , so yeah , you know i know that they just checked your blood sugar now and it was 162 and you know , what ... you know , did you eat before this ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: uh , probably about two hours ago .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay , all right . hey , dragon , show me the diabetes labs . yeah , so your hemoglobin a1c here is is 8 , you know last time we had seen it , it was about 6 and we had n't put you on medications so , um , i think it's something we'll have to talk about , okay ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: you got it .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: um , so let's just talk a little bit about my assessment and my plan for you so for your first problem , this newly diagnosed diabetes . um , you know , i want to continue on the metformin 500 mg twice a day . we'll probably increase that over time .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: mm-hmm .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: i'm gon na go ahead and order hemoglobin a1c for the future okay ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: sure .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: um for your second problem , your anxiety . it sounds like you know you might have , you know , some issues leading into the winter . how do you feel about that ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: well , i'll try something new just to help . if it helps that'd be great .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay , all right , and so for your last ish issue , your- your epilepsy , you know , i think you saw your neurologist about three months ago , you must be due to see her again some time soon ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: i am .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: and we'll just continue you on the keppra , okay ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: sure .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: any questions ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: not at this point , no .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay , um , hey , dragon , finalize the note .

detail_list.summary: John Brooks is a 55-year-old male with a past medical history of anxiety and epilepsy presenting with an abnormal lab finding.
Diabetes
The patient reports poor monitoring of sugar intake due to recent travel and visitors.
The doctor asks how the patient is feeling regarding his elevated blood sugar.
The doctor asks if the patient was prescribed medication for his blood sugar.
The doctor confirms the patient was prescribed metformin.
Anxiety
The doctor asks how the patient is managing his anxiety.
The patient reports his anxiety symptoms have improved as the weekend approaches.
Epilepsy
The doctor asks if the patient has experienced any recent seizures.
The patient reports no seizures in the past few months.
The doctor asks about symptoms including chest pain, shortness of breath, nausea, vomiting, and dizziness.
The patient denies chest pain, shortness of breath, nausea, vomiting, and dizziness.
The doctor asks about recent fevers or chills.
The patient denies fevers and chills.
Physical exam shows clear lungs, regular heart rate and rhythm, and trace pitting edema in the lower extremities.
The doctor asks if the patient received intravenous fluids in the emergency room.
The doctor asks if the patient ate before his blood glucose check.
The patient's hemoglobin A1c is 8.
Continue metformin 500 mg twice daily.
The doctor will order a repeat hemoglobin A1c test.
The doctor asks if the patient is due for a follow-up with his neurologist.
Continue Keppra at the current dosage.
The doctor asks if the patient has any further questions.
